summary: I served as president of the Chamber of Commerce in 1960, right before I ran for mayor in 1961. The Chamber played a key role in pushing for the consolidation of city and county governments in Charlotte and Mecklenburg County during the late 1960s. They established committees to study the idea as early as 1963 or 1964, and by 1968-69, consolidation was a major program in their work. Although the Chamber was the primary driver behind the effort, not all members fully supported the complex charter proposed, and some even gave lip service without real enthusiasm.

Dimensions for Charlotte Mecklenburg was started a little over two years ago as a nonprofit organization to serve the community. I served as chairman for the first two years and still hold a corporate office there. Our role has been to help guide community goals and ensure we live within our budget. Cliff Cameron, who is chairman of the board of First Union Corporation, is currently our chairman, which shows the strong civic-minded leadership involved.

Cliff Cameron was chairman of the board of First Union Corporation and is currently chairman of Dimensions for Charlotte Mecklenburg. He was also chairman of the campaign committee for the consolidation effort in the late 1960s. Cliff was very civic-minded and capable, and he took the failure of the consolidation personally, though I believe he shouldn't have. He had been involved in planning and was on the Chamber committee that proposed consolidation earlier on.

First Union Corporation was connected to the consolidation effort through its chairman, Cliff Cameron, who played a major civic role. As chairman of First Union, Cliff brought leadership to Dimensions for Charlotte Mecklenburg and the consolidation campaign. The corporation itself was not directly involved, but its leadership was influential in local civic matters during the late 1960s and early 1970s.

Charlotte, North Carolina, was the center of the consolidation efforts between city and county governments around 1968-69. As mayor for four terms starting in 1961, I witnessed firsthand the community's satisfaction with existing local government, which contributed to the failure of consolidation. The city had overlapping services with Mecklenburg County, and many believed consolidation was a logical next step, though the proposed charter was too complex for most citizens to accept. Issues like district representation and black community involvement were hot topics during this time.

Mecklenburg County, alongside Charlotte, was involved in the consolidation talks during the late 1960s. I worked closely with Charlie Lowe, then chairman of the County Board of Commissioners, to appoint members to the Charter Commission. The county and city had overlapping services, and many felt one government should take full responsibility, but the community was generally satisfied with the status quo. The consolidation effort aimed to modernize local government but ultimately failed due to lack of crisis and complexity of the proposed changes.

The Charter Commission was appointed by Charlie Lowe and me to draft a new government structure during the consolidation push in 1968-69. We aimed for broad representation to give many community segments a voice, but the commission went beyond simple consolidation to propose a large, modernized governing body with 15 or 16 members. This was too much change for the public, and the districting plan sparked controversy, especially regarding black representation. Though they worked hard, their idealistic approach and complexity contributed to the failure of the consolidation effort.

At the time, Charlotte's City Council was a seven-member body, much smaller than the 15 or 16 members proposed by the Charter Commission during the consolidation effort. The existing council was part of the local government structure that many citizens were satisfied with, which made them reluctant to accept the extensive changes suggested. The idea of district representation was controversial, with concerns about returning to an old ward system and potential political bargaining among district representatives.

Charlie Lowe was chairman of the Mecklenburg County Board of Commissioners when we appointed members to the Charter Commission in the late 1960s. The board was part of the existing local government structure that the consolidation effort sought to reorganize. Like the City Council, the five-member board was well established, and many citizens were satisfied with its performance, which contributed to resistance against the proposed changes. The board worked alongside the city government on overlapping services, which was a key reason for considering consolidation.

Charlie Lowe was chairman of the Mecklenburg County Board of Commissioners during the consolidation discussions around 1968-69. He and I jointly appointed members to the Charter Commission and selected its chairman. Charlie played a significant role in trying to create a broadly representative commission to draft a new government charter, although the effort ultimately failed. His leadership was crucial in bridging city and county interests during that time.
cues: chairman of County Board of Commissioners
